description:
You are a database administrator who has been asked to identify the most common slow SQL statements recorded by the query profiler.

Current environment  
• A plain-text log file already exists at  
  /home/user/db/logs/slow_queries.log

Goal  
1. Determine how many times each *distinct, complete* SQL statement appears in the log file.  
2. Produce a frequency-ordered report file at  
   /home/user/db/reports/slow_query_frequency.txt

Report format requirements (exactly as listed; automated grading will match these rules verbatim):  
• Each line must contain:  
  COUNT␣QUERY_TEXT  
  – COUNT is the integer occurrence count.  
  – Exactly one ASCII space (0x20) must separate COUNT and QUERY_TEXT.  
  – QUERY_TEXT must be the full SQL statement copied verbatim from the log, including the trailing semicolon.  
• The lines must be sorted in *descending* numeric order by COUNT.  
• If two statements have identical counts, their relative order is irrelevant.  
• Do not print any header, footer or blank lines—only the data lines.

Operational constraints  
• Use only standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities that are pre-installed in a typical distribution (e.g., sort, uniq, mkdir, cat, etc.).  
• Create /home/user/db/reports if it does not already exist.  
• Overwrite /home/user/db/reports/slow_query_frequency.txt if it pre-exists.  
• Do not move or modify the original log file.

Success verification (performed automatically)  
• /home/user/db/reports/slow_query_frequency.txt exists.  
• The file is world-readable (0600 ≤ mode ≤ 0666 is acceptable).  
• The file’s content adheres exactly to the format rules above and reflects the correct counts from the provided log.
